title: Latest News from Science Magazine
description: Keyword search result for Alerts: News Weekly Headlines -- New results matching your keyword search query (updated daily).

items.title: After USDA request, Indiana plant biologist locked out of lab by school
items.description: Move comes after Roger Innes complained about the government’s prosecution of Chinese postdocs

items.title: Computer scientist to lead storied DOE lab through ‘exciting and threatening’ AI revolution
items.description: Katherine Yelick will become director of the Department of Energy’s Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory on 1 July

items.title: Trump administration cuts CDC’s key role in global program to stop HIV
items.description: State Department would send PEPFAR money directly to countries, which could then pay fees to health agency for help

items.title: As researchers aim for universal AI disclosure guidelines, the devil is in the details
items.description: Integrity conference attendees debated when and how authors should declare AI use in papers

items.title: Fiber optic cables can eavesdrop on nearby conversations
items.description: Cables used to detect earthquakes can also capture the faint vibrations of speech

items.title: What to do with the passengers on the cruise ship hit by hantavirus?
items.description: Past studies of Andes virus offer scant evidence on transmission risks, and have sparked debate

items.title: In the remote Amazon, locals are saving a giant fish—and helping their villages
items.description: Project has brought income and electricity while protecting wide swaths of tropical forest

items.title: Ancient ice core could help explain mysterious shift in Earth’s ice ages
items.description: Record-setting Antarctic archive reveals sharp swings in carbon dioxide 1 million years ago

items.title: Magic mushroom compound shows promise against cocaine addiction
items.description: Small study that prioritized Black and low-income participants yields “remarkable” results

items.title: AI agents may be skilled researchers—but not always honest ones
items.description: Two high-profile tools have been shown to make up data and “p-hack” their results
